name: Roshan Di Kulfi
why_it_matters: Started by 19-year-old Roshan Lal Soni who arrived from Lahore at Partition. Karol Bagh's oldest shop — kulfi falooda by day, chhole bhature for breakfast, run by the third generation now.
insider_tip: Order both — the kulfi falooda is the headline but locals rate the chhole bhature equally. Skip the ground-floor counter and walk to the open seating at the back; it's faster.
signature_address: 2816, Ajmal Khan Road, Block 34P, Beadonpura, Karol Bagh, New Delhi 110005

name: Sita Ram Diwan Chand
why_it_matters: A one-dish restaurant since 1947 — Lahori chhole with paneer-stuffed bhature, served the same way three generations later. Started as a cycle-cart in Jhula Mandi; the original Paharganj stall is the only one that matters.
insider_tip: Goes morning to mid-afternoon — show up before 1pm or you'll miss the day's bhature. Take the chhole on the side rather than poured over; the bread crisps better.
signature_address: 2243, Rajguru Road, Chuna Mandi, Paharganj, New Delhi 110055

name: Dolma Aunty Momos
signature_dish: steamed chicken momos with chilli garlic chutney
why_it_matters: Where Delhi's momo culture started. Dolma Tsering, a Tibetan refugee from Bylakuppe, set up a stall in Lajpat Nagar in 1994 and democratised momos for the city. The brand now turns ₹60 lakh a month.
insider_tip: Stand at the original Central Market thela, not the chain branches — the difference is the chutney, made fresh on-site. Arrive after 4pm; mornings are spotty.
signature_address: Lajpat Nagar Central Market, opposite Block 4, New Delhi 110024

name: Ghalib Kabab Corner
why_it_matters: Tucked into Nizamuddin Basti by the Markazi Masjid — open till 3am, named after the 19th-century poet Ghalib whose tomb is two lanes away. Shami kebabs ground from minced goat meat, the recipe unchanged for 67 years.
insider_tip: Walk through the dargah lanes after the qawwali on Thursday/Sunday evenings and arrive here past 10pm; the basti energy is the dish, the kebabs are the side. ₹450 feeds two.
signature_address: Shop 57, Ghalib Road, near Lal Mahal, Nizamuddin Basti, New Delhi 110013

name: Karim's (Nizamuddin)
why_it_matters: The 1990s Nizamuddin extension of the Jama Masjid original — same family, slightly tamer spice for the South Delhi expat crowd. Regulars consider it the best sit-down version of Karim's if the Old Delhi traffic is too much.
insider_tip: The Nizamuddin branch is air-conditioned with proper tables (unlike Old Delhi's plastic stools). Spices are noticeably milder — ask for 'Old Delhi style' and the kitchen will adjust.
signature_address: 168/2, Aurobindo Marg, Jha House, Nizamuddin West, New Delhi 110013

name: Pind Balluchi
why_it_matters: Nationwide Punjabi/frontier chain born in Delhi — folk-village interiors, swings instead of chairs at some tables, and a 100-dish menu. Karol Bagh outlet is the local family go-to for Sunday dinner.
insider_tip: Skip the Chinese section on the menu — it's not the strength. Order the gilafi seekh and fish amritsari for a kebab-led meal that doesn't repeat what every Pandara Road kitchen does.
signature_address: 2C/2, New Rohtak Road, Karol Bagh, New Delhi 110005

name: Chache Di Hatti
why_it_matters: Delhi University students' breakfast canteen for half a century — aloo-stuffed bhature ₹80, gone by 2pm. Closes the kitchen as soon as the dough runs out.
insider_tip: Open 9:30am-2pm only; arrive by 11am or settle for plain. The aloo bhatura is the variant locals queue for; plain is for tourists.
signature_address: D-33, Bungalow Road, near Sony Showroom, Kamla Nagar, New Delhi 110007

name: Punjabi By Nature
why_it_matters: The vodka-golgappa shooter started here before going viral nationwide. Mid-range Punjabi chain born in Delhi, still anchored by the Priya Complex flagship in Vasant Vihar.
insider_tip: The vodka golgappa is what tourists order; locals come for the raan-e-mughlai (24-hour-marinated whole leg of lamb). Order it 2 hours ahead if going for dinner.
signature_address: 11, PVR Priya Complex, Vasant Vihar, New Delhi 110057
